metadata.summary: Offload large tool results to filesystem working memory, pass back only paths. Summarize context only when utilization hits 85% AND no offloadable blobs remain.

content.title: Filesystem as Working Memory — Offload Before Summarize
content.summary: Give every long-running agent a write_file / read_file / ls tool surface backed by real disk, S3, or a state-dict virtual FS. Whenever a tool produces a large blob (search dump, full document, raw API response), write it to the FS and pass back only a filename plus a short snippet. Reserve LLM-based context summarization for the moment context hits ~85% AND there is nothing left to offload — file offload preserves information losslessly, summarization does not.

why.p: LangChain's "Deep Agents" pattern and the "Anatomy of an Agent Harness" report converge on a single observation: agents that summarize prematurely lose facts they will need three turns later, while agents that offload to a filesystem keep every fact recoverable on demand. Treating the FS as working memory turns every tool result into a referenceable artifact: cheap to keep, cheap to skip, cheap to re-read at the precision the next step actually needs. The result is a hard upper bound on context size that does not require lossy compression.

list.item: Research agents reading more than 5 documents per task.
Codegen across many files (Claude Code, Aider, OpenHands, Cursor agents).
Any task expected to run more than ~20 turns.
Pipelines where step N's output is consumed by step N+1 — paths flow, not blobs.
Agents that must survive process restart (FS persists, conversation does not).
Short chat sessions under ~10 turns — the FS abstraction adds ceremony with no payoff.
Single-document QA — the document already fits in context once.
Hard real-time loops where every disk write adds unacceptable latency.
Untrusted code execution where a writable FS is itself a security problem.

rule.statement: Any tool whose output exceeds ~2000 tokens MUST write the full output to the agent's filesystem and return only a path plus a short snippet (first ~200 tokens or a structured digest). Summarization of conversation history runs only after context utilization crosses 85% AND no offloadable blobs remain in context.
rule.rationale: File offload is lossless and reversible — the agent can re-read the original at any precision later. Summarization is lossy and one-way; doing it first throws away information the agent might need to recover.

section.name: Why two thresholds
section.p: Two thresholds keep behaviour predictable across short and long runs:

list.item: Per-tool-result threshold (~2000 tokens) — applies on every tool call, prevents single oversized blobs from dominating the window.
Conversation threshold (~85% of context window) — applies once per turn, triggers summarization only when offload alone is no longer enough.

example.note: Result envelope is small and uniform; agent can choose to re-read.

example.code: def web_search(query: str) -> str: return provider.search(query) # 30k tokens straight into context
example.note: One call eats a quarter of the window; chains of these force premature summarization.

antipattern.description: Summarizing the conversation at the first sign of pressure (50-60% utilization) instead of offloading the next tool result.
antipattern.reason: Lossy compression applied while reversible space-savings are still available — the agent loses recoverable facts to save tokens it didn't need to save yet.

section.name: Minimum FS tool surface
section.p: Four tools — write_file, read_file, ls, grep — are sufficient; avoid wrapping every operation in its own tool.

rule.statement: Expose exactly four FS tools to the agent — write_file(path, content), read_file(path, offset?, limit?), ls(path?), grep(pattern, path?) — and route every offload/recall through them.
rule.rationale: Four primitives compose like Unix utilities; adding move/copy/append separately bloats the tool list and makes the agent pick wrong tools (see bundle-vs-split-tools at ~25 tools).

example.note: Default limit forces streaming — the agent never accidentally re-loads a 50k-token file in full.

rule.statement: Use a stable directory taxonomy — search/, docs/, plan/, scratch/ — and let SKILL or system prompt list it; never let the agent invent ad-hoc top-level folders per call.
rule.rationale: A predictable taxonomy makes ls a useful navigation aid; ad-hoc paths force the agent to remember names instead of discovering them.

list.item: search/ — raw search results (one file per query).
docs/ — fetched URLs and document downloads.
plan/ — TODO files, task graphs, intermediate plans.
scratch/ — agent's own notes between turns.

rule.statement: The FS backing store MUST persist into the same checkpointer as conversation state, so resume-after-failure recovers files alongside messages.
rule.rationale: If files survive but messages don't (or vice versa) the agent wakes up with dangling references — pointers to artifacts it can no longer interpret, or summaries of files that no longer exist.

antipattern.description: FS in tmpfs / per-process RAM, conversation in Postgres — process restart leaves the agent with file paths that 404.
antipattern.reason: Memory model splits across two persistence boundaries; agent's references decay independently of its own awareness of them.

section.name: Working-memory anti-patterns
section.p: Three failures: summarizing too early, round-tripping blobs through the model, and losing files on restart.

antipattern.description: Compaction routine fires whenever context utilization crosses ~50%, replacing the conversation with a summary while large tool outputs are still inline.
antipattern.reason: The lossless lever (offload to FS) was still available; the agent burned facts it could have kept.

rule.statement: Compaction must be the LAST resort: only fire when no inline tool result above ~2000 tokens remains AND utilization is past 85%.
rule.rationale: Order of operations matters — offload first, summarize second; reversing the order causes information loss without need.

antipattern.description: Tool reads a file, returns the full content to the model, model emits a tool call to write the same content to a different file.
antipattern.reason: The blob crosses the model boundary twice for no reason — pay the input + output token cost on data the model never needed to "see".

example.note: Operation completes without round-tripping the file content.

antipattern.description: Agent stores files in /tmp or in-memory dict, conversation in durable store; restart leaves dangling paths.
antipattern.reason: Two persistence boundaries — agent's mental model assumes the file still exists because the message that mentions it does.

rule.statement: Conversation checkpoint and FS snapshot must commit atomically (same store, same transaction, or paired with a write-ahead reconciliation step) so resume is consistent.
rule.rationale: An agent recovering with one half of its memory missing produces silent data corruption — paths point to nothing, summaries describe artifacts that never existed.

template.purpose: OpenAI/Anthropic tool definitions for the four FS primitives.

see-also.link: Anatomy of an Agent Harness — Daily Dose of DS.
